---
title: "DESN2003 | Research for Innovation"
subtitle: "Method Roast, Peer Review, and Synthesis"
author: Hongshan Guo
format: 
  revealjs:
    slide-level: 2
    center: true
    slide-number: true
    theme: simple
    width: 1600
    height: 1200
    preview-links: auto
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
    
---

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .case-wall {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(3, minmax(0, 1fr));
  gap: 1rem;
  align-items: stretch;
  margin-top: 1rem;
}

.reveal .case-card {
  text-align: left;
  border-radius: 24px;
  border: 4px solid #1d3557;
  padding: 1.15rem 1.2rem;
  box-shadow: 0 14px 0 rgba(29, 53, 87, 0.12);
}

.reveal .case-a {
  background: #fff1d6;
}

.reveal .case-b {
  background: #e3f4ef;
}

.reveal .case-c {
  background: #fde6e2;
}

.reveal .case-tag {
  display: inline-block;
  margin-bottom: 0.7rem;
  padding: 0.25rem 0.6rem;
  border-radius: 999px;
  background: #1d3557;
  color: #ffffff;
  font-size: 0.58em;
  font-weight: 800;
  letter-spacing: 0.12em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
}

.reveal .case-title {
  margin-bottom: 0.55rem;
  color: #111827;
  font-size: 1.2em;
  font-weight: 800;
  line-height: 1.05;
}

.reveal .case-body {
  color: #1f2937;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.reveal .case-claim {
  margin-top: 0.75rem;
  color: #111827;
  font-size: 0.74em;
  font-weight: 700;
}

.reveal .case-badges {
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  gap: 0.45rem;
  margin-top: 0.9rem;
}

.reveal .case-badge {
  padding: 0.26rem 0.55rem;
  border: 2px solid #1d3557;
  border-radius: 999px;
  background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.85);
  color: #1d3557;
  font-size: 0.54em;
  font-weight: 800;
  letter-spacing: 0.04em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
}

.reveal .case-large {
  max-width: 1100px;
  margin: 0 auto;
}

.reveal .case-large .case-title {
  font-size: 1.75em;
}

.reveal .case-large .case-body {
  font-size: 0.92em;
}

.reveal .case-large .case-claim {
  font-size: 0.95em;
}

.reveal .student-justify {
  text-align: left;
  border-radius: 22px;
  border-left: 10px solid #1d3557;
  background: #ffffff;
  padding: 1rem 1.2rem;
  box-shadow: 0 12px 0 rgba(29, 53, 87, 0.10);
}

.reveal .student-label {
  display: inline-block;
  margin-bottom: 0.7rem;
  padding: 0.24rem 0.58rem;
  border-radius: 999px;
  background: #1d3557;
  color: #ffffff;
  font-size: 0.56em;
  font-weight: 800;
  letter-spacing: 0.08em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
}

.reveal .student-justify p {
  margin: 0 0 0.8rem 0;
  color: #111827;
  font-size: 0.78em;
  line-height: 1.45;
}

.reveal .roast-focus {
  text-align: left;
  border-radius: 22px;
  border: 3px solid #1d3557;
  background: #f7f8fa;
  padding: 1rem 1.05rem;
}

.reveal .roast-focus ul {
  margin: 0.35rem 0 0 1.1rem;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  line-height: 1.45;
}

@media (max-width: 1200px) {
  .reveal .case-wall {
    grid-template-columns: 1fr;
  }
}
</style>
```

# The Runway Is Short Now {background-color="#E9C46A"}

## What This Session Is Actually For

::: {.callout-important icon=false}
If Week 8 was about choosing a defendable method, this session is about turning that method into **usable evidence and usable writing**.
:::

. . .

- **Mar 31**: Professional Discovery presentations begin
- **Apr 7**: no class
- **Apr 14**: Professional Discovery presentations continue + **Doc 0.2** + **Professional Discovery Documentation**
- **Apr 21**: **Doc 1 due** + final presentation sequence begins

If your method is still vague, go back to the Week 8 method paragraph first. Everything here assumes you can already say what data you are collecting and why.

<!-- ## Recapping on Literature Review
- Grades & Comments published on Moodle
- Major reasons that the grades may not appear to be satisfying:
  - formatting is off
  - recounting, synthesis are in, but critical lens are missing
    - question literature if you could
  - if you need any clarifications feel free to reach out to me for time to catch up next week on this during office hour
    - we may arrange in-class seminar session if demands are high enough

## Group Project Example
  - Already posted on Moodle
  - Quick walk-through lookouts:
    - framework
    - contents that are expected
    - just an example so not the final 'solution manual' that you will need to follow
    - typical length that is expected, so nothing major
    - demonstrates how you have leveraged techniques learnt in this class to investigate problems at hand
  - Let's take a quick look -->

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

1. **Diagnose** common methodology failures before they appear in your own draft
2. **Give** focused peer feedback on a research question, literature review paragraph, and method summary
3. **Identify** the next revision priorities for Doc 0.2 and Doc 1
4. **Synthesize** findings into a more defensible argument or product hypothesis

---

## Today's Session Structure

1. **Method Roast 2.0** (25 min) — Diagnose broken research designs
2. **Peer Review Lite** (35 min) — Research question + lit review + method fit
3. **Synthesis / Research → Decision** (30 min) — Turn evidence into a usable claim
4. **Exit Artifacts + Logistics** (10 min)

## Open This Now

- your current research question
- one literature review paragraph you think is strong
- one literature review paragraph you are unsure about
- your current method paragraph, or a 3-4 sentence method summary
- [Week 9 Workshop Sheet (PDF)](data/week9_workshop_sheet.pdf)

::: {.callout-important icon=false}
You are not peer reviewing the whole paper today. That is too broad. We are doing **baby-step peer review** on the parts that most often fail first.
:::
<!-- 
## From previous class, we have five (six) centralized group:
  
  - Fashion | Games (Film-making): possibly expanding to two groups
  - Project Manager
  - Scientists | Academics | Professors
  - Curator | Museum Business
  - Designers

## Task of the day 1

  1. Find your initial team mates;
  2. work out an initial list (excel/Google Sheet) version of top 3 candidates;
  3. You have 15 minutes;
  4. You are not expected to go very deep with social engineering, but 
  5. Selection of candidates should be justified through the excel;
  5. Possible columns can be why you picked them, star project, most recent interview, etc. and preference rank. -->

<!-- ## Time left before initial presentation
:::{.timer #task1 seconds=900}
:::
 -->
<!-- # Methodology (Due 11:59 AM Apr 14th) -->

<!-- ## Circling back to Methodology Examples in Google Drive

- Refer to W8 folder
- Refer to the two papers for methodology framework inspirations aka 'formatting'
  - "Stuck in the stack" & "Air vs. MRT"
  - What level of details did these papers ended up providing in their methodology section?
  - Are they connected somehow to the literature review/background+introduction sections as well?
  - _But what if my methodologies are not natural-science-related?_
- Tutorial/Instruction Manual-like spirit

## Why do people have write a methodology section in research papers?
  - We previously discussed **what** are the typical methodologies out there that supports research as a given.
  - Reserach is ultimately meant for pushing boundaries:
    - 'dissemination'  (dissemination)
      - clarify extent and specifics of the published work
    - '**reproducible**' (repeatable)
      - allow readers to repeat the publication -->

# Workshop 1: Method Roast 2.0 {background-color="#F4A261"}

## Why We Are Doing This Again

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
If you can spot a broken method in somebody else's project in under a minute, you are much less likely to ship the same mistake in your own.
:::

Today the roast is not just for entertainment. It is calibration.

## Group Deliverable: One Roast Verdict Sheet

Use **Section A** of the [Week 9 Workshop Sheet (PDF)](data/week9_workshop_sheet.pdf).

Each group hands in:

- the case label
- the main flaw
- the unsupported claim
- the ethics or access issue, if any
- a defensible revision
- a one-line verdict: `fatal flaw` or `salvageable`

## Roast Prompts

For your assigned case, answer only these:

1. **What is the main flaw?**
2. **What claim is the student trying to make that the method cannot support?**
3. **Is there any ethics, consent, sampling, or access problem?**
4. **What would a defensible revision look like?**

If your comment does not point to a concrete mismatch, it is not useful feedback yet.

## Roast Case Wall

```{=html}
<div class="case-wall">
  <div class="case-card case-a">
    <div class="case-tag">Case A</div>
    <div class="case-title">Too Small to Say That</div>
    <div class="case-body">
      <p><strong>Aim:</strong> AI and creativity in Hong Kong designers</p>
      <p><strong>Plan:</strong> Survey 8 classmates from one studio</p>
    </div>
    <div class="case-badges">
      <span class="case-badge">Scale mismatch</span>
      <span class="case-badge">Convenience sample</span>
      <span class="case-badge">Overclaim</span>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="case-card case-b">
    <div class="case-tag">Case B</div>
    <div class="case-title">Wrong People, Wrong Answer</div>
    <div class="case-body">
      <p><strong>Aim:</strong> What museum visitors want</p>
      <p><strong>Plan:</strong> Interview 2 curators</p>
    </div>
    <div class="case-badges">
      <span class="case-badge">Respondent mismatch</span>
      <span class="case-badge">Perspective gap</span>
      <span class="case-badge">Bad evidence fit</span>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="case-card case-c">
    <div class="case-tag">Case C</div>
    <div class="case-title">Observation Is Not Mind Reading</div>
    <div class="case-body">
      <p><strong>Aim:</strong> Compare pedestrian stress by location</p>
      <p><strong>Plan:</strong> Infer stress from body language in public</p>
    </div>
    <div class="case-badges">
      <span class="case-badge">Over-inference</span>
      <span class="case-badge">Ethics problem</span>
      <span class="case-badge">Weak construct</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

Pick one. Roast hard. Repair precisely.

## Case A: Too Small to Say That

```{=html}
<div class="case-card case-a case-large">
  <div class="case-tag">Case A</div>
  <div class="case-title">Too Small to Say That</div>
  <div class="case-body">
    <p><strong>Aim:</strong> How AI affects creativity in Hong Kong designers</p>
    <p><strong>Plan:</strong> Survey 8 classmates from one studio</p>
    <p class="case-claim"><strong>Claim they want:</strong> Whether AI improves creativity in the design industry</p>
  </div>
  <div class="case-badges">
    <span class="case-badge">Scale mismatch</span>
    <span class="case-badge">Convenience sample</span>
    <span class="case-badge">Overclaim</span>
  </div>
</div>
```

Your job: name the overclaim, then repair the design.

## Case A: Fake Methodology Justification

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}
```{=html}
<div class="student-justify">
  <div class="student-label">Fake Student Doc 0.2</div>
  <p>To investigate how AI affects creativity in Hong Kong designers, I will use a survey because it is an efficient way to collect data from multiple people within a short time. I will distribute the survey to 8 classmates from my own studio because they all have design training and are familiar with AI tools such as Midjourney and ChatGPT.</p>
  <p>I believe this sample is suitable because these students can represent the new generation of Hong Kong designers, and their direct experience with AI can provide accurate information about whether AI improves creativity in design practice. The survey method is also objective because it allows me to present the results clearly in percentages and charts.</p>
</div>
```
:::
::: {.column width="38%"}
```{=html}
<div class="roast-focus">
  <div class="student-label">Roast This Writing</div>
  <ul>
    <li>Where does convenience get disguised as representativeness?</li>
    <li>Which sentence overclaims from students to the whole industry?</li>
    <li>Why is calling the survey "objective" doing too much work here?</li>
    <li>Rewrite one sentence so the scope becomes honest.</li>
  </ul>
</div>
```
:::
:::

## Case B: Wrong People, Wrong Answer

```{=html}
<div class="case-card case-b case-large">
  <div class="case-tag">Case B</div>
  <div class="case-title">Wrong People, Wrong Answer</div>
  <div class="case-body">
    <p><strong>Aim:</strong> What museum visitors want from immersive experiences</p>
    <p><strong>Plan:</strong> Interview 2 curators</p>
    <p class="case-claim"><strong>Claim they want:</strong> Prove what visitors need</p>
  </div>
  <div class="case-badges">
    <span class="case-badge">Respondent mismatch</span>
    <span class="case-badge">Perspective gap</span>
    <span class="case-badge">Bad evidence fit</span>
  </div>
</div>
```

Your job: identify whose voice is missing and what evidence would actually answer the question.

## Case B: Fake Methodology Justification

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}
```{=html}
<div class="student-justify">
  <div class="student-label">Fake Student Doc 0.2</div>
  <p>Based on my literature review, immersive experience is important in museums today. To understand what visitors want, I will conduct semi-structured interviews with 2 curators because curators are professionals who design exhibitions and understand visitor behavior better than ordinary visitors.</p>
  <p>This method is appropriate because curator interviews can provide expert insight into what kinds of immersive strategies are effective and meaningful. It is also practical for this project because curators are easier to access than a large number of museum visitors, while still providing deep and reliable information about visitor needs.</p>
</div>
```
:::
::: {.column width="38%"}
```{=html}
<div class="roast-focus">
  <div class="student-label">Roast This Writing</div>
  <ul>
    <li>Where does "expert" get used to replace the actual target population?</li>
    <li>Which sentence quietly swaps visitor evidence for curator opinion?</li>
    <li>What can curators legitimately tell us, and what can they not prove?</li>
    <li>Rewrite the question or method so they actually fit each other.</li>
  </ul>
</div>
```
:::
:::

## Case C: Observation Is Not Mind Reading

```{=html}
<div class="case-card case-c case-large">
  <div class="case-tag">Case C</div>
  <div class="case-title">Observation Is Not Mind Reading</div>
  <div class="case-body">
    <p><strong>Aim:</strong> Compare pedestrian stress across locations</p>
    <p><strong>Plan:</strong> Infer stress from body language in public spaces</p>
    <p class="case-claim"><strong>Claim they want:</strong> Measure stress without consent</p>
  </div>
  <div class="case-badges">
    <span class="case-badge">Over-inference</span>
    <span class="case-badge">Ethics problem</span>
    <span class="case-badge">Weak construct</span>
  </div>
</div>
```

Your job: separate observable behavior from imagined inner state.

## Case C: Fake Methodology Justification

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}
```{=html}
<div class="student-justify">
  <div class="student-label">Fake Student Doc 0.2</div>
  <p>To compare stress levels in different pedestrian environments, I will use non-participant observation in public space. I will record visible indicators such as posture, walking speed, facial expression, and body movement in order to identify whether pedestrians appear stressed in different locations.</p>
  <p>This method is efficient because observation allows me to collect natural data without interrupting people. Since the study takes place in public space, individual consent is not necessary. By comparing visible stress indicators across sites, I will be able to identify which environments are more stressful for pedestrians.</p>
</div>
```
:::
::: {.column width="38%"}
```{=html}
<div class="roast-focus">
  <div class="student-label">Roast This Writing</div>
  <ul>
    <li>Which phrases pretend inner emotion is directly observable?</li>
    <li>Where does "public space" get used as an ethics shortcut?</li>
    <li>What behavior could actually be recorded without overclaiming?</li>
    <li>If the student truly wants stress, what extra method would be needed?</li>
  </ul>
</div>
```
:::
:::

## Roast Flow

1. **Read the case silently** (2 min)
2. **Fill Section A as a group** (8 min)
3. **Prepare one sharp verdict sentence** (2 min)
4. **Share back** (about 1 min per group)

**If you finish early:** choose a second case and do a lightning roast on the back of the sheet.

## The Failure Modes We Keep Seeing

- the claim is much larger than the sample can support
- the wrong people are being asked the wrong question
- the method collects description but the paper wants causation
- ethics, consent, or access problems are discovered too late
- there is data collection, but no credible analysis plan

# Workshop 2: Peer Review Lite {background-color="#A8DADC"}

## This Is Not Full Peer Review

We are not reviewing the whole paper.

We are testing four things only:

- is the research question actually clear?
- is there at least one paragraph that goes beyond summary?
- is there at least one paragraph that still only summarizes?
- does the current method actually match the question?

## Pair Deliverable: One Peer Review Sheet + One Revision Memo

Use **Section B** of the [Week 9 Workshop Sheet (PDF)](data/week9_workshop_sheet.pdf).

The reviewer must produce:

- a one-sentence summary of the writer's argument
- two strengths
- two revision priorities

The writer must produce:

- a short revision memo with the first three changes they will make

## Bring Only These Four Things

Share only:

1. your current research question
2. one literature review paragraph you think is strong
3. one literature review paragraph you are unsure about
4. your method paragraph, or a 3-4 sentence method summary

That is enough for a useful first-round review.

## Review Rules

- `No grammar policing first.` This round is about argument and method fit.
- `No vague praise.` "Looks good" is useless.
- `No reviewing everything.` Focus on the four prompts only.
- `Name the mismatch.` If something feels off, say exactly where.

## Step 1: Silent Read + Marking (6 min)

As the reviewer:

- underline the clearest research question language
- mark the strongest analytical sentence with a star
- circle the paragraph that is still mostly summary
- put a question mark where the method no longer fits the question

## Step 2: Write Feedback, Not Vibes (10 min)

Complete these prompts on the worksheet:

- `Your project is asking...`
- `Your strongest analytical move is...`
- `This paragraph is still mostly summary because...`
- `Your method stops matching the question when...`
- `Two revision priorities are...`

## What Strong Feedback Sounds Like

| Weak | Useful |
|:-----|:-------|
| "Needs more detail." | "You interview curators, but your research question is about visitors. That is a respondent mismatch." |
| "Good paragraph." | "This paragraph cites three sources, but it never tells the reader what pattern or disagreement matters." |
| "Method unclear." | "You say you will observe behavior, but later you make a claim about feelings that observation alone cannot support." |

## Step 3: Writer Revision Memo (8 min)

After getting feedback, the writer fills in:

- `The clearest part of my draft is...`
- `The biggest mismatch or weakness is...`
- `The first three changes I will make before Doc 0.2 / Doc 1 are...`

This is the part that turns peer review into actual revision.

## If You Brought Almost Nothing

Do not panic.

Use:

- your research question
- one sentence from your notes that tries to state a finding
- a rough method summary

You can still do a useful peer review at that scale.

# Synthesis Activity {background-color="#FFE66D"}

## Using the Synthesis Canvas (25 min)

**The challenge:** You have findings from multiple sources. How do you bring them together?

- Qualitative themes from interviews
- Quantitative patterns from surveys
- Insights from literature

**The tool:** [Synthesis Canvas (PDF)](data/synthesis_canvas.pdf)

---

## Activity: Complete Your Synthesis Canvas

**Step 1: What Data Do You Have?** (3 min)

Fill in Section 1 of the canvas:
- What qualitative data? (interviews, observations, open-ended responses)
- What quantitative data? (survey scores, analytics, measurements)
- How much of each?

---

## Activity: Qualitative Synthesis (10 min)

**Step 2: Pull Key Quotes**

From your interviews/observations, identify 3-5 powerful quotes that capture key insights.

**Step 3: Group Into Themes**

Cluster related quotes. Name each theme. Note how many participants expressed it.

| Theme | Codes Included | # of Participants |
|:------|:---------------|:------------------|
| Theme 1: _________ | _________ | ___/___ |
| Theme 2: _________ | _________ | ___/___ |

---

## Activity: Triangulation Check (7 min)

**Step 4: Do Qual and Quant Agree?**

For each finding, check:

| Finding | Qual Evidence | Quant Evidence | Agreement? |
|:--------|:--------------|:---------------|:-----------|
| _________ | _________ | _________ | Yes / No / Partial |

**If they disagree:** That's interesting data. Write down why they might differ.

---

## Activity: The "So What?" (5 min)

**Step 5: Why Should Anyone Care?**

For your top 2-3 findings, write one sentence explaining the implication:

| Finding | So What? |
|:--------|:---------|
| _________ | This means... / This suggests... / Designers should consider... |

**If you can't answer "so what?" — the finding isn't ready for Doc 1.**

---

## Architecture/Urban Example: Synthesizing Mixed Methods Data

**Research question:** Does greenery in Hong Kong's elevated walkways affect pedestrian stress levels?

| Data Type | Source | Key Finding |
|:----------|:-------|:------------|
| **Qualitative** | 10 walking interviews | "The plants make it feel less like a tunnel" (7/10) |
| **Quantitative** | Heart rate variability (N=45) | 12% lower stress markers in green sections vs. bare sections |
| **Observation** | Pedestrian counts | 23% more people paused/lingered in green sections |

**Triangulation:** All three methods converge — greenery correlates with lower stress and changed behavior.

**So what?** Urban designers should prioritize vegetation in elevated walkway design; the effect is measurable, not just aesthetic preference.

# Research → Decision Framework {background-color="#4ECDC4"}

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Applied Research (Builder-Maker) Focus

This section is specifically for students on the **Applied Research track** who are building design case studies or product recommendations. If you're on the Frontier Research track, this framework shows how industry applies research — useful context even if your output is a traditional paper.
:::

## The Product Builder's Challenge

You've done your research. You found the behavioral science. Now what?

**Academic output:** "Our findings suggest that social comparison mediates the relationship between like-checking frequency and anxiety (β = 0.38, p < .001)."

**Product question:** "Should we hide like counts? Will it actually help? How will we know?"

This section bridges that gap.

## The Translation Problem

| What Research Gives You | What Product Needs |
|:------------------------|:-------------------|
| Correlations & themes | Causal hypotheses |
| General principles | Specific feature specs |
| "This is happening" | "If we do X, Y will happen" |
| Academic language | Stakeholder-ready framing |

**Your job:** Turn "what we know" into "what we should build and why we expect it to work."

## The Research → Decision Canvas

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Grab This Now
**[Research to Decision Canvas (PDF)](data/research_to_decision_canvas.pdf)** — Fill this in as we walk through the examples. Page 1 is blank, page 2 is a worked example.
:::

| Component | Question | Your Answer |
|:----------|:---------|:------------|
| **Scientific Foundation** | What does behavioral science say about this problem? | _____________ |
| **Product Hypothesis** | If we build [feature], then [outcome] because [mechanism] | _____________ |
| **Expected Effect** | How big a change do we expect? What's the baseline? | _____________ |
| **Success Metric** | How will we measure if it worked? | _____________ |
| **Risk if Wrong** | What happens if our hypothesis is wrong? | _____________ |
| **Validation Plan** | How will we test before full launch? | _____________ |

## Worked Example: Instagram Hidden Likes

**Research finding:** Social comparison theory (Festinger, 1954) + your qualitative data showing 9/12 participants check likes to compare with friends.

| Component | Answer |
|:----------|:-------|
| **Scientific Foundation** | Social comparison drives anxiety; visible metrics enable comparison; removing visibility should reduce comparison triggers |
| **Product Hypothesis** | If we hide like counts from viewers, then comparison-driven anxiety will decrease, because users can't compare their posts to others' |
| **Expected Effect** | Baseline: 67% report checking to compare. Target: <40% report comparison motivation after hiding. |
| **Success Metric** | Self-reported comparison frequency (survey); anxiety scale (GAD-7); qualitative interviews |
| **Risk if Wrong** | Users might feel less validated, reducing posting motivation; engagement metrics might drop |
| **Validation Plan** | A/B test with 10% of users for 4 weeks; exit survey + 10 interviews |

## Case Study: Duolingo's Streak Feature

**The science:** Loss aversion (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979) — people are more motivated to avoid losing something than to gain something of equal value.

**The translation:**

| Component | Duolingo's Answer |
|:----------|:------------------|
| **Scientific Foundation** | Loss aversion: losing a streak feels worse than gaining a day |
| **Product Hypothesis** | If we visualize consecutive-day streaks, users will practice daily to avoid "losing" their streak |
| **Expected Effect** | Daily active users should increase; session frequency should stabilize |
| **Success Metric** | DAU/MAU ratio; 7-day retention; streak length distribution |
| **Risk if Wrong** | Users might feel punished by broken streaks and churn |
| **Validation Plan** | Introduced incrementally; added "streak freeze" as safety valve |

**Result:** Streaks became Duolingo's core retention mechanism.

## Case Study: Spotify Wrapped

**The science:** Self-presentation theory (Goffman, 1959) — people curate identity through what they share; nostalgia increases emotional engagement.

**The translation:**

| Component | Spotify's Answer |
|:----------|:-----------------|
| **Scientific Foundation** | Sharing music = identity signaling; year-end reflection = nostalgia; social proof drives adoption |
| **Product Hypothesis** | If we create shareable, personalized year-in-review content, users will spread it organically, driving brand awareness and emotional connection |
| **Expected Effect** | Viral social sharing in December; increased premium conversions post-Wrapped |
| **Success Metric** | Social shares; hashtag volume; December premium signups vs. baseline |
| **Risk if Wrong** | Users might find it creepy or ignore it |
| **Validation Plan** | Started with simple stats; iterated based on what got shared most |

**Result:** Wrapped became Spotify's biggest annual marketing moment — built on behavioral science, not ad spend.

## When Your Research Says "Don't Build It"

Sometimes research reveals your hypothesis is wrong. That's valuable.

**Signals to reconsider:**

- Literature shows the mechanism doesn't work the way you thought
- Qualitative data shows users don't actually have the problem you assumed
- Comparable products tried it and failed (with documented reasons)
- The effect size in existing research is too small to matter

**What to do:**

1. Document why you're not building it (prevents revisiting the same dead end)
2. Look for adjacent opportunities the research revealed
3. Share with stakeholders — "We saved X months by researching first"

## What Product Research Output Looks Like

**Not a paper.** A decision document.

**Structure:**

1. **The Opportunity** (1 paragraph): What problem are we solving? For whom?
2. **What We Learned** (1 page): Key findings from research — qual themes, quant patterns, literature insights
3. **Our Hypothesis** (1 paragraph): If we build X, then Y, because Z
4. **Recommendation** (1 paragraph): Build / Don't build / Test first
5. **Validation Plan** (if building): How we'll know if we're right
6. **Appendix**: Raw data, quotes, methodology details

**Length:** 2-3 pages + appendix. Readable in 10 minutes.

## The Screenshot-Worthy Slide

::: {.callout-tip}
## Research → Decision in One Sentence

**"We found [scientific principle] in the literature and [pattern] in our data, which suggests that if we build [feature], users will [outcome] — and we'll measure success by [metric]."**
:::

**Example:**

> "We found that loss aversion (Kahneman & Tversky) predicts people work harder to protect what they have than to gain something new. Our interviews confirmed users hate 'losing progress.' This suggests that if we add a visible streak counter, users will practice daily to avoid breaking it — and we'll measure success by 7-day retention rate."

---

## Artifact Output: What You Leave With

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Today's Deliverables

**From Workshop 1: Method Roast 2.0**

- one completed **roast verdict sheet** per group
- one sharp diagnosis of a broken method
- one defensible revision of that method

**From Workshop 2: Peer Review Lite**

- one completed **peer review sheet**
- one reviewer summary of the argument
- one writer revision memo with the next three changes

**From Synthesis / Frontier Research:**

- Draft Synthesis Canvas with at least 2 themes + triangulation check

**From Research → Decision / Applied Research:**

- Completed Research to Decision Canvas with hypothesis and validation plan

**These feed directly into Doc 0.2 and Doc 1.**
:::

# Logistics: Interview Project Reminder

## Group/Individual Interview Project

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Quick Reminder

The interview-based project (group or individual) gives you practice with:

- Professional outreach and networking
- Primary data collection through interviews
- Synthesizing insights from first-hand sources

**Choose group or individual based on your preferences.** Check Moodle for current deadlines and submission guidelines.
:::
